title: Neonatal Opioid Withdrawal (Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome) in a 2-Day-Old Newborn Exposed to Maternal Opioid-Use-Disorder Treatment: Recognizing Withdrawal Signs, Assessing with Finnegan / Eat-Sleep-Console, NON-PHARMACOLOGIC Comfort Care FIRST (Rooming-In, Skin-to-Skin, Swaddling, Low Stimulation, Frequent Small Feeds, Breastfeeding When Appropriate), Pharmacologic Morphine for Severe Symptoms, and Non-Judgmental Maternal Support with a Plan of Safe Care
subtitle: NEONATAL ABSTINENCE SYNDROME (NAS) / NEONATAL OPIOID WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (NOWS) is the constellation of withdrawal signs in a newborn who was exposed in utero to substances (most often OPIOIDS — including a mother appropriately treated with medication for opioid use disorder/MOUD such as methadone or buprenorphine, as well as illicit opioids). ONSET TIMING depends on the substance's half-life: SHORT-acting opioids (e.g., heroin) → signs within ~24-72 hours; LONG-acting opioids (METHADONE, buprenorphine) → signs may be DELAYED up to ~5-7 days (important for discharge timing/observation). SIGNS span systems: CNS (central) — IRRITABILITY, a HIGH-PITCHED CRY, TREMORS, HYPERTONIA/increased muscle tone, hyperactive/exaggerated Moro reflex, difficulty being consoled, disturbed/short SLEEP, and (severe) SEIZURES; GASTROINTESTINAL — POOR/UNCOORDINATED FEEDING, frantic/excessive sucking, regurgitation/vomiting, LOOSE/watery STOOLS, dehydration, and poor weight gain; AUTONOMIC/metabolic/respiratory — SWEATING, temperature instability/fever, frequent YAWNING and SNEEZING, nasal stuffiness, tachypnea, mottling, and skin EXCORIATION (from rubbing/movement). ASSESSMENT/SCORING: the FINNEGAN Neonatal Abstinence Scoring Tool quantifies the severity of signs at intervals to guide care/treatment; the newer, function-based EAT, SLEEP, CONSOLE (ESC) approach asks whether the infant can EAT adequately (e.g., feed well/take an age-appropriate volume), SLEEP undisturbed for ≥1 hour, and be CONSOLED within ~10 minutes — ESC PRIORITIZES non-pharmacologic care and the infant's function and has been shown to reduce unnecessary medication and length of stay. NON-PHARMACOLOGIC CARE IS FIRST-LINE and the mainstay (the heart of nursing for NAS): (1) ROOMING-IN — keep the mother and infant TOGETHER (mother as the primary caregiver) rather than routinely separating to the NICU, which reduces withdrawal severity, medication need, and length of stay and supports bonding; (2) SKIN-TO-SKIN (kangaroo) contact; (3) SWADDLING (snug) and gentle, slow handling; (4) a LOW-STIMULATION ENVIRONMENT — DIM lights, QUIET, minimal/clustered handling, avoid sudden movements/noise, protect sleep; (5) SOOTHING measures — holding, rocking, a pacifier (non-nutritive sucking), responding promptly to cues; (6) FEEDING — small, FREQUENT feeds (these infants have high caloric needs and uncoordinated/frantic feeding), with higher-calorie feeds if weight gain is poor; (7) BREASTFEEDING is ENCOURAGED when NOT contraindicated — it is recommended for mothers who are stable on MOUD (methadone/buprenorphine) and not using illicit drugs, are HIV-negative, and have no other contraindication; breastfeeding reduces NAS severity, supports bonding, and only small amounts of MOUD pass into milk (do NOT abruptly stop breastfeeding, which could precipitate worsening). PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT is added when non-pharmacologic measures are insufficient and the infant has SEVERE/persistent symptoms (e.g., high scores, inability to eat/sleep/console, poor weight gain, dehydration, or seizures): an OPIOID is first-line — oral MORPHINE (or methadone; buprenorphine is also used) titrated to control symptoms and then WEANED gradually; ADJUNCTS such as CLONIDINE (or phenobarbital, especially for non-opioid/polysubstance or as an adjunct) may be added; the goal is to control symptoms enough that the infant can EAT, SLEEP, and be CONSOLED and gain weight, then wean. SUPPORTIVE CARE: meticulous SKIN/DIAPER care for loose stools/excoriation (frequent changes, barrier cream), monitor WEIGHT/hydration/intake-and-output and temperature, safe positioning, and seizure precautions if severe. MATERNAL + FAMILY-CENTERED, NON-JUDGMENTAL care: treat the mother with respect and WITHOUT stigma (maternal OUD is a medical disease, and MOUD is appropriate, recommended treatment — NOT a reason to blame or separate); INVOLVE the mother in caregiving and soothing (empowering, bonding, and therapeutic for the infant), support her MOUD/treatment and (when appropriate) breastfeeding, and keep mother and baby together when safe. PLAN OF SAFE CARE: under federal law (CAPTA/CARA), a substance-exposed infant prompts a NOTIFICATION to the child-welfare/development-of-services system and the creation of a PLAN OF SAFE CARE addressing the health/substance-treatment needs of the infant AND mother/caregiver — this is intended as a SUPPORTIVE, service-connecting process (linking to treatment, home visiting, early intervention), NOT inherently a punitive abuse finding (distinguish from a child-abuse report); engage SOCIAL WORK. DISCHARGE/FOLLOW-UP: SAFE-SLEEP education, feeding support, maternal treatment continuity, early-intervention/developmental follow-up (NAS infants are at risk for feeding and developmental issues), and connection to community resources. NURSING ROLE: recognize NAS signs and assess with Finnegan/ESC; deliver and prioritize NON-PHARMACOLOGIC comfort care (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, low stimulation, frequent feeds, breastfeeding when appropriate); administer/monitor pharmacologic therapy for severe symptoms; provide supportive skin/feeding/hydration care; support the mother-infant dyad non-judgmentally and involve her in care; and coordinate the Plan of Safe Care, social work, and follow-up

patient.name: Baby T.
patient.code: Full newborn care; mother is the primary caregiver (rooming-in); goal = manage neonatal withdrawal with non-pharmacologic comfort care first, support feeding/weight, keep the dyad together, and connect to services via a Plan of Safe Care
patient.admitDate: A 2-day-old term newborn whose mother is in treatment for opioid use disorder (stable on buprenorphine MOUD during pregnancy). The infant is developing signs of NEONATAL OPIOID WITHDRAWAL (NAS/NOWS): irritability, a high-pitched cry, tremors, increased tone, frequent yawning/sneezing, poor coordinated feeding, and loose stools, with difficulty settling. The infant is being assessed with NAS scoring (Finnegan / Eat-Sleep-Console) and managed with non-pharmacologic comfort care first (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, low stimulation, frequent small feeds, breastfeeding as appropriate), with pharmacologic morphine reserved for severe/uncontrolled symptoms, plus non-judgmental maternal support and a Plan of Safe Care.
patient.room: Mother-baby (couplet) unit, rooming-in; low-stimulation environment; interdisciplinary team (neonatology/pediatrics, mother-baby nurse, lactation, social work, maternal addiction-treatment team); Plan of Safe Care + community-service linkage in process

37.2°C; NAS assessed with the FINNEGAN tool and/or the EAT-SLEEP-CONSOLE (ESC) function-based approach (can the infant EAT adequately, SLEEP ≥1 hour, be CONSOLED within ~10 min?); scoring + function guide whether non-pharmacologic care is sufficient or pharmacologic treatment is needed; weight/intake-output + skin (excoriation) monitored

labs.v: Baby T. is a 2-day-old term newborn whose mother is in treatment for OPIOID USE DISORDER, stable on BUPRENORPHINE (medication for OUD/MOUD) during pregnancy — appropriate, recommended treatment. The infant is developing NEONATAL ABSTINENCE SYNDROME (NAS) / NEONATAL OPIOID WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (NOWS): IRRITABILITY, a HIGH-PITCHED CRY, TREMORS, increased muscle tone with an exaggerated Moro, frequent YAWNING/SNEEZING and nasal stuffiness, POOR/UNCOORDINATED feeding with frantic sucking, LOOSE stools, mild temperature instability, and difficulty being consoled. ONSET TIMING is clinically important and depends on the substance half-life: SHORT-acting opioids (heroin) cause signs within ~24-72 hours, while LONG-ACTING opioids (METHADONE, BUPRENORPHINE) can cause DELAYED onset/peak up to ~5-7 days — so infants of mothers on MOUD require an adequate OBSERVATION period before discharge (signs may appear/peak after the typical 48-hour newborn stay). NAS signs span CNS (irritability/high-pitched cry/tremors/hypertonia/poor sleep/seizures), GI (poor feeding/vomiting/loose stools/poor weight gain/dehydration), and autonomic/metabolic/respiratory (sweating/temperature instability/yawning/sneezing/tachypnea/skin excoriation). SOCIAL/CONTEXT: mother engaged in addiction treatment + prenatal care; supportive; the care team approaches her NON-JUDGMENTALLY (maternal OUD is a medical disease; MOUD is correct treatment — NOT grounds for blame or routine separation). The nurse's priorities: recognize and assess the withdrawal (Finnegan/Eat-Sleep-Console), deliver NON-PHARMACOLOGIC comfort care FIRST (the mainstay), reserve pharmacologic treatment for severe/uncontrolled symptoms, support feeding/weight/skin, keep and support the mother-infant DYAD, and coordinate a Plan of Safe Care + follow-up.
labs.r: NAS/NOWS = in-utero (usually opioid) exposure withdrawal: CNS (irritability/high-pitched cry/tremors/hypertonia/seizures), GI (poor feeding/loose stools/poor weight gain), autonomic (sweating/yawning/sneezing/temperature instability). Onset: short-acting opioids 24-72h; LONG-acting (methadone/buprenorphine) delayed up to 5-7 days → observe before discharge. Mother on MOUD = appropriate (non-judgmental). RN role: recognize + assess + non-pharm care first + support the dyad.

labs.v: STANDARDIZED ASSESSMENT guides care and the decision to treat. The FINNEGAN NEONATAL ABSTINENCE SCORING TOOL (or modified versions) assigns points to a list of withdrawal signs across CNS, GI, and autonomic/metabolic/respiratory domains, scored at regular intervals (often every 3-4 hours, after feeds); higher/persistently elevated scores indicate more severe withdrawal and historically triggered escalating pharmacologic treatment. The NEWER, function-based EAT, SLEEP, CONSOLE (ESC) approach reframes assessment around the infant's FUNCTION and the adequacy of non-pharmacologic care, asking three questions: can the infant EAT adequately (feed well / take an age-appropriate volume, ~≥1 oz or breastfeed effectively), SLEEP undisturbed for ≥1 HOUR, and be CONSOLED within ~10 MINUTES (with non-pharmacologic measures and caregiver support)? ESC EMPHASIZES maximizing NON-PHARMACOLOGIC care and parental presence, and using medication only when the infant cannot eat/sleep/console despite optimized comfort measures; ESC has been associated with LESS pharmacologic treatment and SHORTER length of stay than scoring-driven approaches. Either way, ASSESSMENT is paired with monitoring of WEIGHT (and feeding adequacy), INTAKE/OUTPUT and hydration (loose stools), and SKIN integrity (excoriation), and is performed in a way that does not over-stimulate the infant (assess with minimal disturbance, ideally during routine cares). The nurse uses the chosen tool consistently, ensures non-pharmacologic care is OPTIMIZED before attributing inability to console to a need for medication, and communicates findings to the team to guide treatment/weaning decisions. THIS INFANT is assessed with Finnegan/ESC, with the team prioritizing whether optimized comfort care allows her to eat, sleep, and console before considering pharmacologic therapy.
labs.r: Assess NAS with the FINNEGAN scoring tool (points for CNS/GI/autonomic signs, scored q3-4h) and/or the function-based EAT-SLEEP-CONSOLE (ESC) approach (can the infant EAT well, SLEEP ≥1h, be CONSOLED within ~10 min with non-pharm care?) — ESC maximizes non-pharm care + parental presence and reduces medication/LOS. Pair with weight/feeding/hydration/skin monitoring; assess with minimal stimulation. RN role: consistent scoring + optimize non-pharm care before medicating.

labs.v: NON-PHARMACOLOGIC CARE is FIRST-LINE and the foundation of NAS management — it reduces withdrawal severity, the need for medication, and length of stay, and it is the heart of nursing care: (1) ROOMING-IN — keep the MOTHER and INFANT TOGETHER with the mother as the primary caregiver (rather than routine separation to the NICU); rooming-in is consistently associated with LOWER NAS severity, LESS medication, shorter stays, and better bonding. (2) SKIN-TO-SKIN (kangaroo) contact — calming and regulating. (3) SWADDLING — snug swaddling (with safe technique) contains the infant and reduces tremor-related distress. (4) LOW-STIMULATION ENVIRONMENT — DIM the lights, keep it QUIET, minimize and CLUSTER handling/care, avoid sudden noise/movement, and PROTECT SLEEP (these infants are easily overstimulated, which worsens withdrawal). (5) SOOTHING — gentle holding/rocking, a PACIFIER for non-nutritive sucking, vertical rocking/swaying, and responding PROMPTLY to early cues before escalation. (6) FEEDING — offer SMALL, FREQUENT feeds (these infants have increased caloric needs and may feed frantically or ineffectively); use higher-calorie feeds if weight gain is inadequate; pace feeds and burp frequently (vomiting/loose stools are common). (7) BREASTFEEDING is ENCOURAGED when NOT contraindicated — it is RECOMMENDED for mothers who are STABLE on MOUD (methadone/buprenorphine), are NOT using illicit drugs, are HIV-NEGATIVE, and have no other contraindication; breastfeeding REDUCES NAS severity (small amounts of MOUD in milk provide a gentle taper), supports bonding, and should NOT be abruptly stopped (abrupt weaning can worsen withdrawal); support the mother with lactation help. Throughout, INVOLVE the mother as the primary comfort provider (empowering and therapeutic). These measures are continued EVEN IF pharmacologic treatment becomes necessary. THIS INFANT was cared for with rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, a low-stimulation environment, frequent small feeds, and breastfeeding (mother eligible), and consoled more easily, fed better, and slept longer as a result.
labs.r: NON-PHARMACOLOGIC care is first-line + the mainstay: ROOMING-IN (keep mother + baby together — reduces severity/medication/LOS), SKIN-TO-SKIN, SWADDLING, LOW-STIMULATION environment (dim/quiet/cluster care/protect sleep), soothing (rocking/pacifier/prompt response), small FREQUENT feeds (higher-calorie if needed), and BREASTFEEDING when not contraindicated (mother stable on MOUD, no illicit drugs, HIV-negative — reduces NAS; don't abruptly stop). Involve the mother. RN role: optimize non-pharmacologic comfort + feeding.

labs.v: PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT is ADDED when NON-pharmacologic measures (optimized) are INSUFFICIENT and the infant has SEVERE or persistent withdrawal — indicated by an inability to EAT/SLEEP/CONSOLE despite comfort care (ESC), high/persistent FINNEGAN scores, poor weight gain or DEHYDRATION from feeding difficulty/loose stools, or SEIZURES. FIRST-LINE pharmacologic therapy for opioid withdrawal is an OPIOID — oral MORPHINE (most common) or METHADONE (longer-acting); buprenorphine is also used in some centers — titrated UP to control symptoms (so the infant can eat, sleep, and console and gain weight), maintained, and then WEANED gradually as the infant stabilizes. ADJUNCTIVE agents include CLONIDINE (an alpha-2 agonist) and PHENOBARBITAL (often reserved for non-opioid/polysubstance exposure or as an adjunct when an opioid alone is insufficient). KEY PRINCIPLE: non-pharmacologic care is NOT stopped when medication is started — it continues alongside, and the medication is titrated to the lowest effective dose and weaned. SUPPORTIVE CARE throughout: (a) SKIN/DIAPER care — frequent diaper changes and a barrier cream for the loose, frequent stools that cause perianal EXCORIATION, and protect skin from rubbing/tremor-related abrasion; (b) NUTRITION/HYDRATION — monitor WEIGHT daily, intake/output, and hydration (high caloric needs + GI losses), supplement calories as needed; (c) TEMPERATURE regulation; (d) SAFE POSITIONING and, if severe, SEIZURE PRECAUTIONS; (e) gentle, clustered care to avoid overstimulation. The treatment GOAL is symptom control sufficient for the infant to feed, sleep, console, and grow — then taper. THIS INFANT received non-pharmacologic care first; if her symptoms had remained severe/uncontrolled, oral morphine would be titrated (with continued comfort care) and later weaned, with meticulous skin and feeding/weight support throughout.
labs.r: Pharmacologic treatment is ADDED (not first) for SEVERE/uncontrolled withdrawal (can't eat/sleep/console, high scores, poor weight gain/dehydration, seizures): first-line OPIOID — oral MORPHINE (or methadone), titrated then WEANED; adjuncts clonidine/phenobarbital. CONTINUE non-pharmacologic care alongside. Supportive: skin/diaper care for excoriation, monitor weight/hydration/I&O, temperature, seizure precautions if severe. RN role: administer/titrate/wean meds + continue comfort + supportive care.

labs.n: Maternal/dyad support + Plan of Safe Care; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: MATERNAL + FAMILY-CENTERED, NON-JUDGMENTAL CARE is essential and therapeutic: maternal OUD is a chronic MEDICAL disease and MOUD (methadone/buprenorphine) in pregnancy is APPROPRIATE, recommended treatment that protects mother and fetus — the mother is NOT to be blamed, shamed, or routinely separated from her infant. INVOLVE the mother as the PRIMARY caregiver and comfort provider (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, soothing, feeding) — this reduces NAS severity AND strengthens BONDING and maternal confidence; support her ongoing MOUD/treatment and (when appropriate) BREASTFEEDING; provide stigma-free education and emotional support. PLAN OF SAFE CARE: under federal law (CAPTA, amended by CARA — the Comprehensive Addiction and Recovery Act), a substance-exposed infant prompts a NOTIFICATION to the child-welfare/service system and the development of a PLAN OF SAFE CARE that addresses the health and substance-use-treatment needs of BOTH the infant and the mother/caregiver and family — this is intended as a SUPPORTIVE, SERVICE-CONNECTING process (linking to maternal treatment, home visiting, early intervention, and supports), NOT inherently a punitive child-abuse finding (it should be DISTINGUISHED from a report of suspected abuse/neglect, though a separate concern would still be reported); engage SOCIAL WORK. DISCHARGE/FOLLOW-UP: provide SAFE-SLEEP education (back to sleep, firm flat surface, no soft bedding — especially important as families manage a fussy infant), feeding support, ensure maternal treatment continuity, and arrange EARLY-INTERVENTION/developmental and pediatric FOLLOW-UP (NAS infants are at risk for feeding difficulties and developmental concerns) plus community resources. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the infant was NOT routinely separated from the mother / sent to the NICU when rooming-in was appropriate (rooming-in is preferred); non-pharmacologic comfort care was prioritized FIRST (not jumping straight to medication); the mother was NOT shamed/blamed or told to stop her MOUD; breastfeeding was NOT inappropriately withheld (she met criteria) NOR abruptly stopped (which worsens withdrawal); the environment was NOT bright/loud/overstimulating; and the Plan of Safe Care was framed as SUPPORTIVE service-linkage (not conflated with a punitive abuse report). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: maternal engagement in MOUD + prenatal care; a supportive, involved mother kept with her infant (rooming-in/skin-to-skin/breastfeeding); optimized non-pharmacologic care reducing severity/medication/LOS; appropriate observation for delayed onset (buprenorphine); meticulous feeding/skin/weight support; non-judgmental, stigma-free care strengthening the dyad; a Plan of Safe Care connecting mother + infant to services; and developmental follow-up. EXPECTED outcome: withdrawal is controlled (the infant eats, sleeps, consoles, and gains weight), pharmacologic therapy (if needed) is weaned, the dyad bonds, and both are connected to ongoing treatment/support. NURSING ROLE: recognize + assess NAS (Finnegan/ESC); deliver non-pharmacologic comfort care first (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, low stimulation, frequent feeds, breastfeeding when appropriate); administer/monitor/wean pharmacologic therapy for severe symptoms while continuing comfort care; provide supportive skin/feeding/hydration care; support and involve the mother non-judgmentally; and coordinate the Plan of Safe Care, social work, safe-sleep teaching, and follow-up.
labs.r: Maternal/dyad care is non-judgmental: OUD is a disease + MOUD is correct treatment; keep mother + baby together, involve her as primary caregiver, support MOUD + breastfeeding. PLAN OF SAFE CARE (CAPTA/CARA) = SUPPORTIVE service-linkage for infant + mother (NOT inherently a punitive abuse report); social work. Discharge: safe sleep, feeding support, maternal-treatment continuity, early-intervention/developmental + pediatric follow-up. Negatives confirm correct practice (rooming-in not separation, non-pharm first, no shaming/MOUD-stopping, breastfeeding not withheld/abruptly stopped, low stimulation). RN role: dyad support + service coordination.

nursesNote: Mother-baby (couplet) unit, day of life 2 — Baby T., term newborn; mother in OUD treatment, stable on BUPRENORPHINE (MOUD) during pregnancy. Developing NEONATAL OPIOID WITHDRAWAL (NAS/NOWS): irritability, HIGH-PITCHED CRY, tremors, increased tone + exaggerated Moro, frequent YAWNING/SNEEZING + nasal stuffiness, POOR uncoordinated feeding/frantic sucking, LOOSE stools, mild temp instability, hard to console. Onset fits buprenorphine (LONG-acting → may appear/peak up to ~5-7 days → adequate observation before discharge). NON-JUDGMENTAL approach (maternal OUD = medical disease; MOUD = appropriate treatment — no blame/routine separation). ASSESSMENT: NAS scored with FINNEGAN and/or EAT-SLEEP-CONSOLE (ESC: can she EAT adequately, SLEEP ≥1h, CONSOLE within ~10 min with non-pharm care?); assessed with minimal stimulation; monitor weight/I&O/hydration/skin (excoriation). NON-PHARMACOLOGIC CARE FIRST (mainstay): ROOMING-IN (mother primary caregiver — reduces severity/medication/LOS) + SKIN-TO-SKIN + SWADDLING + LOW-STIMULATION environment (dim, quiet, clustered/minimal handling, protected sleep) + soothing (rocking, pacifier, prompt response to cues) + SMALL FREQUENT feeds (higher-calorie if poor weight gain; pace + burp) + BREASTFEEDING ENCOURAGED (mother stable on buprenorphine, HIV-negative, no illicit drugs → not contraindicated; reduces NAS; do NOT abruptly stop) → infant consoles more easily, feeds better, sleeps longer. PHARMACOLOGIC treatment RESERVED for severe/uncontrolled symptoms (can't eat/sleep/console despite optimized comfort care, high scores, poor weight gain/dehydration, seizures): oral MORPHINE (opioid, first-line) titrated → weaned; adjunct clonidine/phenobarbital (esp. polysubstance); CONTINUE non-pharm care alongside. SUPPORTIVE: meticulous skin/diaper care + barrier cream for loose-stool excoriation; monitor weight daily + I&O + hydration; temperature; seizure precautions if severe. MATERNAL/DYAD: supported mother non-judgmentally + involved her in soothing/feeding (empowering + bonding) + maintained her MOUD + supported breastfeeding. PLAN OF SAFE CARE developed (CAPTA/CARA — SUPPORTIVE service linkage for infant + mother: maternal treatment, home visiting, early intervention; distinct from a punitive abuse report) + social work. DISCHARGE/FOLLOW-UP: SAFE-SLEEP education (back to sleep, firm flat, no soft bedding), feeding support, maternal-treatment continuity, early-intervention/developmental + pediatric follow-up, community resources. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT routinely separate to NICU (rooming-in preferred), did NOT jump to medication first, did NOT shame the mother or stop her MOUD, did NOT withhold or abruptly stop appropriate breastfeeding, did NOT overstimulate, did NOT conflate the supportive Plan of Safe Care with a punitive abuse report. Dyad bonding strong; family-centered, stigma-free care.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: mother-baby (couplet) unit, rooming-in; term newborn with neonatal opioid withdrawal (NAS/NOWS); maternal OUD on buprenorphine MOUD
DIAGNOSIS: neonatal abstinence syndrome / neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome; in-utero opioid (MOUD) exposure
CODE STATUS: full newborn care; mother is the primary caregiver
ASSESSMENT/MONITORING:
Score withdrawal with the Finnegan tool and/or the Eat-Sleep-Console (ESC) approach at intervals (e.g., after feeds); assess with minimal stimulation
Monitor daily weight, intake/output and hydration (loose stools, feeding adequacy), temperature, and skin integrity (excoriation)
Observe for an adequate period before discharge given delayed onset/peak with long-acting opioids (methadone/buprenorphine, up to ~5-7 days)
NON-PHARMACOLOGIC CARE (FIRST-LINE / mainstay):
Rooming-in — keep mother and infant together with the mother as primary caregiver (avoid routine separation)
Skin-to-skin contact; snug swaddling; gentle, slow, clustered handling
Low-stimulation environment: dim lights, quiet, minimize handling, protect sleep, avoid sudden noise/movement
Soothing: holding/rocking, pacifier (non-nutritive sucking), prompt response to early cues
Feeding: small, frequent feeds; higher-calorie feeds if poor weight gain; pace and burp
Breastfeeding: encourage if not contraindicated (mother stable on MOUD, not using illicit drugs, HIV-negative); support with lactation; do NOT abruptly stop breastfeeding
Involve the mother as the primary comfort provider
PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT (for severe/uncontrolled symptoms):
Indications: inability to eat/sleep/console despite optimized non-pharmacologic care, high/persistent scores, poor weight gain/dehydration, or seizures
First-line: oral morphine (or methadone) titrated to symptom control, then weaned; adjuncts clonidine or phenobarbital (esp. non-opioid/polysubstance)
Continue non-pharmacologic care alongside medication; titrate to lowest effective dose and wean as the infant stabilizes
SUPPORTIVE CARE:
Meticulous skin/diaper care (frequent changes, barrier cream) for loose-stool excoriation; protect skin from abrasion
Monitor weight/hydration/nutrition (high caloric needs); temperature regulation; safe positioning; seizure precautions if severe
MATERNAL/FAMILY-CENTERED + SERVICES:
Non-judgmental, stigma-free care; support maternal MOUD/treatment continuity; involve and empower the mother; support bonding

Develop a Plan of Safe Care (CAPTA/CARA) connecting the infant AND mother/family to services (treatment, home visiting, early intervention) — supportive, not punitive; engage social work; distinguish from a child-abuse report
DISCHARGE/FOLLOW-UP:
Safe-sleep education (back to sleep, firm flat surface, no soft bedding); feeding support; maternal treatment continuity
Early-intervention/developmental follow-up + pediatric follow-up; community resources; ensure adequate observation before discharge

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this newborn with withdrawal?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Signs of neonatal opioid withdrawal — irritability, high-pitched cry, tremors, hypertonia, frequent yawning/sneezing, poor uncoordinated feeding, and loose stools — in an opioid-exposed newborn
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing the NAS/NOWS picture drives assessment + care. RN role: recognize withdrawal signs.

opts.text: Onset depends on the substance: long-acting opioids (methadone/buprenorphine) can cause delayed onset/peak up to ~5-7 days, so adequate observation before discharge is needed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — delayed onset with long-acting opioids affects observation/discharge timing. RN role: observe adequately before discharge.

opts.text: Assess with a standardized tool (Finnegan and/or Eat-Sleep-Console), monitoring whether the infant can eat, sleep ≥1 hour, and be consoled — with minimal stimulation during assessment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — standardized, function-based assessment guides care/treatment. RN role: consistent scoring + minimal-stimulation assessment.

opts.text: Non-pharmacologic comfort care is first-line — rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, low stimulation, soothing, and frequent small feeds
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — non-pharmacologic care is the mainstay and reduces severity/medication/LOS. RN role: prioritize comfort care.

opts.text: Breastfeeding is encouraged when not contraindicated (mother stable on MOUD, not using illicit drugs, HIV-negative) and should not be abruptly stopped
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — breastfeeding reduces NAS severity + supports bonding when appropriate. RN role: support eligible breastfeeding; don't abruptly stop.

opts.text: Reserve pharmacologic treatment (oral morphine first-line, titrated then weaned) for severe symptoms not controlled by optimized comfort care; continue comfort care alongside
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — medication is added (not first) for severe/uncontrolled withdrawal. RN role: pharmacologic treatment for severe symptoms + continue non-pharm care.

opts.text: Support the mother non-judgmentally, keep the dyad together, and coordinate a Plan of Safe Care (supportive service linkage) + skin/feeding/hydration support
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — non-judgmental dyad support + Plan of Safe Care + supportive care. RN role: dyad support + service coordination.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine for the newborn. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Routinely separate the infant to the NICU, place under bright lights for observation, and start morphine immediately as the first intervention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Rooming-in (keeping the dyad together) is preferred and reduces severity/medication/LOS; bright lights/overstimulation worsen withdrawal; and pharmacologic treatment is reserved for severe symptoms after optimizing non-pharmacologic comfort care first.

opts.text: Tell the mother she caused this and instruct her to stop her buprenorphine and stop breastfeeding immediately
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + harmful. Maternal OUD is a medical disease and MOUD is appropriate treatment; shaming her is counterproductive; stopping her MOUD is dangerous; and abruptly stopping appropriate breastfeeding can worsen the infant's withdrawal. Provide non-judgmental support.

opts.text: NAS/NOWS reflects in-utero (usually opioid) exposure; onset is earlier with short-acting opioids (24-72h) and delayed (up to ~5-7 days) with long-acting opioids (methadone/buprenorphine)
opts.rat: CORRECT — etiology + onset timing. Foundation for recognition + observation.

opts.text: Assessment uses Finnegan scoring and/or the function-based Eat-Sleep-Console approach, which maximizes non-pharmacologic care and reduces medication/length of stay
opts.rat: CORRECT — assessment frameworks. Foundation for guiding care.

opts.text: Non-pharmacologic care (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, low stimulation, soothing, frequent feeds) is first-line and reduces severity, medication need, and length of stay
opts.rat: CORRECT — non-pharmacologic mainstay principle. Foundation for management.

opts.text: Breastfeeding is encouraged for mothers stable on MOUD without contraindications and reduces NAS severity; it should not be abruptly discontinued
opts.rat: CORRECT — breastfeeding principle. Foundation for feeding decisions.

opts.text: Pharmacologic treatment (first-line oral morphine, titrated then weaned; adjuncts clonidine/phenobarbital) is added for severe/uncontrolled symptoms while continuing comfort care
opts.rat: CORRECT — pharmacologic principle. Foundation for escalation when needed.

opts.text: Maternal OUD is a medical disease and MOUD is appropriate treatment; care is non-judgmental, keeps the dyad together, and uses a Plan of Safe Care for supportive service linkage (not inherently punitive)
opts.rat: CORRECT — non-judgmental dyad + Plan of Safe Care principle. Foundation for family-centered care.

opts.text: Overstimulation (bright lights, loud noise, frequent handling) soothes the withdrawing infant and should be maximized
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Overstimulation WORSENS withdrawal; a LOW-stimulation environment (dim, quiet, minimal/clustered handling, protected sleep) is therapeutic.

opts.text: Pharmacologic treatment should be the immediate first-line intervention for every opioid-exposed newborn regardless of symptom severity
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Non-pharmacologic comfort care is first-line; medication is reserved for severe/uncontrolled symptoms (can't eat/sleep/console, high scores, poor weight gain, seizures) after optimizing comfort care.

opts.text: Optimize non-pharmacologic comfort care (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, low stimulation, soothing) and assess withdrawal with Finnegan/ESC
opts.rat: #1 — Foundational/first-line. Optimized comfort care + assessment guide everything and often control symptoms. RN role: non-pharmacologic care + assessment.

opts.text: Support feeding/nutrition/hydration — small frequent feeds, breastfeeding when appropriate, monitor weight/I&O (high caloric needs + loose stools)
opts.rat: #2 — High. Feeding adequacy + hydration are central to NAS function/recovery. RN role: feeding + hydration support.

opts.text: Administer/titrate/wean pharmacologic treatment (oral morphine) for severe symptoms not controlled by comfort care, continuing non-pharmacologic care
opts.rat: #3 — When indicated. Medication controls severe symptoms; comfort care continues. RN role: pharmacologic management for severe symptoms.

opts.text: Provide supportive skin/diaper care for excoriation and monitor temperature/seizure risk
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Skin protection + monitoring prevent complications. RN role: supportive care.

opts.text: Support the mother non-judgmentally, keep the dyad together, and coordinate the Plan of Safe Care + follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Important + ongoing. Dyad support + service coordination sustain outcomes. RN role: maternal support + Plan of Safe Care + follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this newborn with NAS.

opts.text: Room the infant in with the mother, provide skin-to-skin, swaddle snugly, and keep the environment dim and quiet with minimal/clustered handling
opts.rat: Core non-pharmacologic, first-line care. RN role: rooming-in + comfort + low stimulation.

opts.text: Assess withdrawal with Finnegan/Eat-Sleep-Console and monitor weight, intake/output, hydration, and skin integrity
opts.rat: Standardized assessment + supportive monitoring. RN role: scoring + monitoring.

opts.text: Offer small, frequent feeds and encourage breastfeeding (mother stable on buprenorphine, HIV-negative, not using illicit drugs)
opts.rat: Appropriate feeding + eligible breastfeeding (reduces NAS). RN role: feeding/breastfeeding support.

opts.text: Reserve oral morphine (titrated then weaned) for severe symptoms not controlled by optimized comfort care, continuing non-pharmacologic care alongside
opts.rat: Correct pharmacologic escalation for severe symptoms. RN role: medicate for severe symptoms + continue comfort.

opts.text: Provide barrier cream and frequent diaper changes for loose-stool excoriation; monitor temperature and weight
opts.rat: Supportive skin/nutrition care. RN role: skin protection + monitoring.

opts.text: Support the mother non-judgmentally, involve her in soothing/feeding, continue her MOUD, and develop a Plan of Safe Care with social work
opts.rat: Non-judgmental dyad support + service coordination. RN role: maternal support + Plan of Safe Care.

opts.text: Routinely separate the infant to the NICU and limit maternal contact to reduce stimulation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — rooming-in (keeping the dyad together with the mother as caregiver) reduces severity/medication/LOS; routine separation is counterproductive.

opts.text: Start oral morphine immediately as the first intervention for every opioid-exposed newborn
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — non-pharmacologic care is first-line; medication is reserved for severe/uncontrolled symptoms after optimizing comfort care.

opts.text: Place the infant under bright lights with frequent handling and noise for close observation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — overstimulation worsens withdrawal; provide a low-stimulation (dim, quiet, minimal-handling) environment.

opts.text: Instruct the mother to stop her buprenorphine and abruptly stop breastfeeding, and tell her she caused the baby's condition
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/harmful — stopping appropriate MOUD is dangerous, abruptly stopping eligible breastfeeding worsens withdrawal, and shaming is counterproductive. Provide non-judgmental support.

opts.text: Treat the Plan of Safe Care as a punitive child-abuse report and threaten the mother with losing custody to motivate her
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the Plan of Safe Care is a SUPPORTIVE, service-connecting process (CAPTA/CARA), not inherently a punitive abuse report; threatening/coercing the mother is inappropriate and harmful.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Optimize non-pharmacologic comfort care (rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddle, dim/quiet environment, soothe) and assess withdrawal with Finnegan/ESC
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. First-line comfort care + assessment often control symptoms and guide care. RN role: comfort care + assessment.

opts.text: Support feeding (small frequent feeds, breastfeeding if appropriate) and monitor weight/intake/output/hydration
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Feeding/hydration are central to function and recovery. RN role: feeding + hydration.

opts.text: If symptoms remain severe/uncontrolled, administer and titrate pharmacologic treatment (oral morphine) while continuing comfort care
opts.rat: #3 — Third, when indicated. Medication controls severe symptoms with continued comfort care. RN role: pharmacologic management.

opts.text: Provide supportive skin/diaper care for excoriation and monitor temperature/seizure risk
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Supportive care prevents complications. RN role: skin/temperature/seizure monitoring.

opts.text: Support the mother non-judgmentally, coordinate the Plan of Safe Care + social work, provide safe-sleep teaching, and arrange follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/discharge. Dyad support + service coordination + follow-up sustain outcomes. RN role: maternal support + Plan of Safe Care + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: With rooming-in, skin-to-skin, swaddling, a low-stimulation environment, soothing, and frequent feeds, the infant consoles more easily, feeds better, and sleeps longer between cares
opts.rat: Optimized non-pharmacologic comfort care improving function (eat/sleep/console). Expected, desired response.

opts.text: Breastfeeding (mother eligible on buprenorphine) is supported and not abruptly stopped; the infant's withdrawal severity and feeding improve
opts.rat: Appropriate breastfeeding reduces NAS severity + supports bonding. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Despite optimized comfort care the infant cannot eat/sleep/console with high scores and poor weight gain, so oral morphine is started and titrated while comfort care continues, and symptoms come under control
opts.rat: Appropriate escalation to pharmacologic treatment for severe symptoms (with continued comfort care) controlling withdrawal. Correct, improving handling.

opts.text: The infant feeds well, gains weight, consoles, and sleeps; pharmacologic therapy (if used) is weaned, and the dyad is connected to services and developmental follow-up at discharge
opts.rat: Recovery + safe, supported discharge with follow-up. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the infant was routinely separated to the NICU under bright lights with frequent handling and no maternal involvement; withdrawal worsened and medication need/length of stay increased
opts.rat: ⚠️ Separation + overstimulation worsen withdrawal and increase medication/LOS. Demonstrates why rooming-in + low stimulation + maternal involvement are first-line.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — morphine was started immediately as the first intervention without optimizing comfort care, and the infant received unnecessary medication and a prolonged stay
opts.rat: ⚠️ Jumping to medication first (skipping non-pharmacologic care) leads to unnecessary medication/longer stays. Demonstrates non-pharmacologic-first management.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the mother was told to stop her buprenorphine and abruptly stop breastfeeding and was shamed; she disengaged from care and the infant's withdrawal worsened
opts.rat: ⚠️ Stopping appropriate MOUD/abruptly stopping eligible breastfeeding + shaming worsens outcomes and disengages the mother. Demonstrates the need for non-judgmental dyad support.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the long-acting-opioid-exposed infant was discharged at 48 hours before adequate observation; withdrawal peaked at home day 5 with poor feeding and dehydration
opts.rat: ⚠️ Premature discharge missed the delayed peak of long-acting-opioid withdrawal. Demonstrates the need for adequate observation before discharge.

opts.text: Early on, before comfort measures take full effect, the infant's withdrawal signs are present and essentially unchanged while the nurse optimizes the environment and feeding
opts.rat: Before non-pharmacologic measures take full effect, an unchanged baseline is expected; continue optimizing comfort care and reassess function (eat/sleep/console).

opts.text: At follow-up the infant is thriving and gaining weight, the mother remains in treatment and bonded, and both are connected to early-intervention and community services
opts.rat: Sustained recovery + dyad bonding + service linkage. Standard ideal longer-term outcome.
